Crude oil prices are falling, with the sharpest declines in the spot market and near-dated futures contracts, even as global petroleum inventories deplete rapidly to the lowest for the time of year since 2008.

First, a general comment: I can't stand random uncontextualized transit count figures and "oh you probably aren't counting this one"isms. Show me a time series and a list of which transits your counting specifically (to cross check against my list) or gtfo.

- there has been a notable uptick, as reported by Kpler, beginning late-May
- that dark transit uptick has been offset by far fewer "official" Iranian route passages
- traffic remains massively throttled, headline counts little changed

Highlights from EIAs weekly report:
Crude stocks fell for a seventh straight week by 7.2mb to a 2022 seasonal low. 
Cushing fell to 21.6 mb, near but still above operational minimum levels around 20 mb
SPR inventories fell 7.9 mb, holding just above the 40-year low from 2023
Production saw a meaningful increase of 92 kb/d to 13.8 mb/d  
Crude export slowed 1mb/d to 4.8 mb/d, with total crude and fuel exports falling to nine-week low at 12.5 mb/d.
Refinery utilisation held at a 2018 high just over 95%, with jet fuel production hitting a fresh record
Diesel stockpiles slumped to a 1990 seasonal low
Implied demand for gasoline (4-wk avg.) held steady while distillate rose 109 kb/d

US CPI came in broadly in line with expectations with the negative supply chock lifting headline inflation to 4.2%, its highest level in three years, while core inflation edged up to 2.9%. The figures underpins the market's current focus on persistent inflation risks, driven by higher energy prices, and the prospect of higher-for-longer interest rates. 
On a 3-month annualised based the headline #inflation reached 8%.

The report I published yesterday, when fear was dominant again and everything felt dark. Enjoy the read 👇
June 10, 2026 US Consumer Price Index CPI Report

MarketMaestro

21 hours ago
The market is locked onto Consumer Price Index, CPI, data. This data set is seen as the most critical macroeconomic crossroads that will determine market direction on the eve of the first Federal Open Market Committee, FOMC, meeting under new Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh, amid the energy shocks created by escalating geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East, new tariff walls in supply chains, and unprecedented structural cost increases created by artificial intelligence investments.

General Consensus and Expectation Ranges

According to analyst estimates collected by FactSet, headline CPI is expected to rise 0.5% month over month in May and climb from the previous month’s 3.8% level to 4.2% year over year. Core CPI, which excludes volatile items such as food and energy and is the main focus of monetary policy, is expected to rise 0.3% month over month and reach 2.9% year over year.

Goldman Sachs: The institution, which has largely abandoned rate cut expectations because of labor market resilience, expects Core Personal Consumption Expenditures, PCE, inflation to gradually fall to 2.1% by December.

JPMorgan Chase: The institution’s Chief US Economist Michael Feroli draws attention to the fragile impact of the May CPI data on market psychology. According to Feroli, an upside inflation surprise above expectations would completely shatter the inflation is transitory narrative in markets and pull forward the timing of the first rate hike. JPMorgan’s models show that in such an inflationary scenario, the probability of the Fed hiking rates by September 2026 would exceed 50%, and that a second hike would start to be priced by December. I have seen this institution be wrong many times.

Morgan Stanley: Matt Hornbach says the May CPI data to be released will be spicier, and that the pressure created by high energy and AI infrastructure costs will keep inflation alive. On the other hand, Chief Global Economist Seth Carpenter expects global growth to be 3.2% in 2026 and inflationary pressures to soften as oil prices decline toward $90 by year end.

Deutsche Bank: The bank offers one of the most aggressive headline inflation estimates on Wall Street, 4.29%, based on its projection of a sharp 6.8% monthly increase in gasoline prices specifically for CPI.

Citi and Barclays: Citi analysts confirm that headline inflation will jump to 4.2%, while saying the real key question is whether there will be a month over month slowdown in core CPI.

Real time market data, platforms such as Zillow, and independent rent indexes show that new rent increases across the US have largely stopped, and that real rent costs are easing as housing supply increases and landlords activate aggressive incentives such as free months to find tenants. However, the survey based OER calculation method used by the BLS follows market realities with a 6 to 12 month lag. As Jim Bianco from Bianco Research also noted, a statistical anomaly caused by the government shutdown threat last September continues to artificially inflate shelter figures. This methodological lag will continue to cause shelter inflation to remain high on paper in the May CPI report, despite cooling in the real economy.

For the May CPI to be released in June, the T-1 signal in Truflation systems produced warnings before the official print, indicating that live market pricing in key components points to upward pressure and that the official print is likely to surprise. However, Truflation Chief Data Officer Oliver Rust says: High figures from the BLS are entirely due to instant jumps in energy and methodological statistical errors in shelter; despite the strong surface appearance in the labor market, the underlying core economy is actually softening rapidly.

Kevin Warsh will chair the first FOMC meeting of his career on June 16-17.

Warsh is expected to completely remove the rate cut friendly bias toward easing from the official statement and shift to a neutral stance policy position. The new Fed Chair is expected to adopt a strategy of keeping rates high for longer by citing stubborn CPI data, in order not to repeat the past inflation is transitory mistake.

Market Scenarios After the Data

If headline inflation approaches Deutsche Bank’s extreme estimates and pushes toward 4.3%, and especially if core goods, because of tariffs, and shelter, because of lagged OER effects, surprise to the upside, the initial market reaction could be extremely destructive.

If the data comes exactly in line with the FactSet median and Bank of America estimates, markets will quickly calm down after the data volatility and shift focus to the FOMC meeting one week later and Kevin Warsh’s communication.

If headline data falls below 4.0% and core inflation records an increase lower than 0.2%, this could create excitement in markets. But this probability currently looks difficult.

The Cleveland Fed’s dynamic model expects headline inflation at 4.05%, below consensus, and projects a more stable path for core inflation at 0.23% month over month and 2.83% year over year. On the other hand, Truflation’s real time data fell to a low level of 1.83%. They see OER and shelter items in BLS data as structural noise.

🦇Batman Rational Forecast for May 2026 CPI

• Headline CPI, monthly: 0.38% to 0.42% range. Direct reflections of the geopolitical energy shock will be effective, but consensus seems overly pessimistic.

• Headline CPI, yearly: 4.0% to 4.1% range. Slightly below the market consensus of 4.2%, closer to the Cleveland Fed’s 4.05% expectation.

• Core CPI, monthly: 0.20% to 0.25% range.

• Core CPI, yearly: flat around 2.8%.

Ultimately, although the data that will hit screens on the morning of June 10 will look uncomfortable at the headline level because of high energy costs and lagged shelter effects, when looking into core details, it may carry signs of structural cooling. The new Federal Reserve leadership under Kevin Warsh will have to take this geopolitical noise in headline figures into account while building its communication strategy, but in the background, it will be aware that restrictive monetary policy is creating a disinflationary gravitational pull in real consumer demand.

In this scenario, if the market chooses tomorrow to strip out the energy and shelter noise in the headline and buy the cooling in the core, we may see a controlled upward move. However, a print at or above the consensus boundary could send hedging costs in the market flying ahead of Kevin Warsh. Because since this is the new Fed Chair’s first meeting, nobody wants to take risk in this uncertainty.

🇮🇷⚔️🇺🇸Oil Surges Above $93 as Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Raising Fears of Global Economic Shock

Global energy markets were jolted on Thursday after Iran officially announced the closure of the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, sending crude oil prices soaring above $93 per barrel and triggering fears of a new inflationary wave across the world economy.

The move follows escalating tensions in the Gulf and warnings from Tehran that commercial vessels transiting the narrow waterway could face military action. The Strait of Hormuz, which links the Persian Gulf to international markets, handles roughly a quarter of the world's seaborne oil trade and a significant share of global liquefied natural gas exports.

Energy traders reacted immediately to the announcement, driving benchmark crude prices sharply higher as concerns mounted over potential supply disruptions. Analysts warned that prolonged restrictions on shipping through the strait could remove millions of barrels of oil from global markets and place severe strain on already fragile supply chains.

Industry observers estimate that a substantial volume of crude oil and LNG shipments is now effectively trapped, creating uncertainty for major importers across Europe and Asia. Shipping companies and insurers are reportedly reassessing risk exposure in the region, with expectations that maritime insurance premiums will surge if tensions continue to escalate.

The energy shock is also expected to fuel inflationary pressures worldwide. Higher oil prices typically translate into increased transportation, manufacturing and logistics costs, raising prices for consumers and businesses alike. Economists cautioned that central banks could face renewed pressure to maintain elevated interest rates for longer than previously anticipated in order to contain energy-driven inflation.

Financial markets have begun shifting toward defensive positioning. Investors are increasing exposure to energy producers expected to benefit from sustained higher crude prices, while demand for traditional safe-haven assets such as gold and short-term government bonds has strengthened.

Meanwhile, sectors heavily dependent on fuel costs, including airlines, shipping operators and energy-intensive manufacturers, are facing heightened scrutiny from investors concerned about shrinking profit margins.

Governments are closely monitoring developments, with market participants watching for any coordinated release of strategic petroleum reserves by Western nations. Such measures could help stabilize supplies, but analysts warn that a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz would have far-reaching consequences for global trade, inflation and economic growth.

Each new round of strikes sets a higher floor on the geopolitical risk premium. The market stopped treating Hormuz as a temporary closure weeks ago — it is now pricing a structural shift. If traders expected near-term resolution, forward curve backwardation would be collapsing. It is not. US crude inventories fell for a seventh consecutive week.

The volatility pattern is structurally different from prior supply shocks. WTI at $90 and Brent at $94 already embed a risk premium, but the physical market is tighter underneath. US crude inventories have drawn for seven straight weeks, and SPR releases are cushioning the ceiling, not restoring the floor. When SPR drawdowns slow, the underlying supply deficit becomes visible fast. The next catalyst isn't a diplomatic headline — it's inventory math.

🚨America’s oil stockpile situation is becoming one of the most misunderstood stories in energy markets.

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), Cushing, Oklahoma, and California’s isolated fuel system are increasingly moving to the center of the macro conversation. 🧵

We at Piggo’s Trading Desk believe investors should pay closer attention to oil inventories than oil prices.

Prices tell you what happened.

Inventories tell you what may happen next.

The U.S. energy system depends on several critical layers:

🛢 Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) 🛢 Cushing, Oklahoma 🛢 Gulf Coast refining 🛢 California PADD 5 inventories 🛢 Commercial storage hubs

Together they function as the nation's energy shock absorbers.

Cushing, Oklahoma is particularly important.

Why?

Because it serves as the primary delivery hub for WTI crude futures.

In many ways: 🛢 Cushing is the heartbeat of America's oil market.

When inventory conditions become stressed at Cushing:

⚠️ futures markets react ⚠️ refinery planning changes ⚠️ logistics flows adjust ⚠️ volatility increases

That is why energy traders watch it obsessively.

California’s PADD 5 system represents another critical vulnerability.

Unlike much of the United States:

🚫 limited pipeline connectivity 🚫 isolated refining network 🚫 unique fuel specifications 🚫 heavy import dependence

California effectively operates as an energy island.

One of the biggest debates emerging today involves the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

Historically the SPR was designed as:

🛡 emergency protection 🛡 geopolitical insurance 🛡 supply disruption buffer

rather than a routine market-management tool.

The SPR was created following: ⛽ 1973 oil shocks ⛽ OPEC disruptions ⛽ national-security concerns

The goal was resilience.

Not day-to-day market intervention.

The political temptation, however, is obvious.

Whenever fuel prices rise:

Presidents face pressure to release barrels.

This has occurred under multiple administrations from both parties. (Trum more so than most)

The SPR story spans: Republican administrations and Democratic administrations

The incentives remain remarkably similar.

Every SPR release provides short-term relief.

But it also creates long-term questions:

❓ When should inventories be replenished? ❓ At what price? ❓ How much buffer is enough?

Energy security ultimately depends on inventory resilience.

Because inventories buy time.

Time to: ⚡ reroute supplies ⚡ repair infrastructure ⚡ respond to disruptions ⚡ stabilize markets

Another critical issue:

The U.S. still consumes enormous amounts of petroleum products.

Fuel remains essential for:

🚛 trucking 🚂 rail ✈ aviation 🚜 agriculture ⚡ backup generation 🏭 industry

California remains particularly exposed.

Any disruption impacts:

🚗 commuters 🚛 freight corridors 🚜 Central Valley agriculture ✈ airports ⚓ ports

all simultaneously.

This is why inventory levels matter so much.

Energy shortages rarely stay confined to energy.

They spread into:

🍎 food 📦 retail 🏗 construction 🏥 healthcare 🚚 logistics

Another important angle:

Inventories affect psychology.

When consumers believe supplies are tight:

⛽ panic buying increases ⛽ hoarding emerges ⛽ volatility rises

The perception of scarcity can become almost as powerful as scarcity itself.

The broader macro backdrop makes this story even more important.

Today we already have:

⚠️ elevated deficits ⚠️ supply-chain vulnerabilities ⚠️ geopolitical tensions ⚠️ infrastructure constraints

Historically, major energy shocks often emerge when:

📉 inventories fall 📈 demand rises ⚠️ supply disruptions appear

all at the same time.

That combination creates the most dangerous environment.

The lesson for investors:

Oil stockpiles are not simply numbers on a government report.

They represent:

🛡 resilience 🛡 flexibility 🛡 strategic capacity 🛡 economic stability

The real question moving forward is not merely:

“Where is oil today?”

The real question is:

“How much flexibility remains if something goes wrong tomorrow?”

Energy markets ultimately reward preparation.

And inventories are preparation.

When inventories are strong: ✅ shocks are manageable

When inventories become strained: ⚠️ every disruption matters more

Final takeaway:

The oil-stockpile story is really a resilience story.

Whether examining: 🛢 SPR levels 🛢 Cushing inventories 🛢 California fuel systems 🛢 refinery capacity

the key issue remains the same:

How much buffer exists between normal operations and crisis?

That question may become one of the most important macro themes of the next several years.
